transcript: What if everyone in a company had stock ownership, not just to the assistant treasurer level, but to the factory floor, distribution centers? How might employees' lives be impacted? In 2015, we invested in a company called CHI Overhead Doors. Out of 800 employees, only 18 had stock ownership. So that means when we bought the business, most people got nothing and just went back to work and a small handful made many millions of dollars. Employee engagement scores were absolutely dismal.  So day one, all 800 employees were granted stock ownership. So they would participate as the value the company went up through dividends along the way and then at the end when the business was sold. But this was about much more than just stock ownership. Employees were educated about the business. They were kept informed. Once a quarter, we got everyone together and we talked openly about what was working and what was not and where the business was headed. And we shared our financial information transparently.  When the company was sold, we gathered everyone in our main manufacturing plant in central Illinois with our most tenured factory workers earning six and a half times their annual income or a half a million dollars. So this was life impacting amounts of money. This wasn't a gift. It didn't happen by a chance they made it happen and it did change the community. $340 million of wealth was injected into the community and that it's forever changed.

transcript: There are three big ideas associated with meaningfulness in life. The first big idea is coherence. The world needs to feel coherence. After winter comes spring, after spring comes summer, you get up in the morning, you brush your teeth, you have breakfast, you get the kids off the school, you go to work, those little routines make the world feel knowable, predictable. Second big idea associated with meaningfulness in life, second big idea, purpose.  Purpose. Now sometimes people use the term purpose as a synonym for meaning in life. And that's not how I mean it here. Purpose is a way of thinking about what your life can be and should be in the future. When you get up in the morning you know what you need to do. Here's where the drive for personal achievement is strongest. It's clearest. Now the third big idea by many considered to be the most important is significance. Now the way I like to think about significance.  is the sense that you can transcend yourself, that you are more than what you are right now, that you will continue to matter into the future beyond this moment. I think the human need and pursuit of meaning is both beautiful and profound because when you have meaning in your life, that means that someone else gave you the opportunity to participate in the story that is not your own. transcript: I've spent nearly two decades observing what makes people luckier than others and trying to help people increase their luck. So how do you catch the winds of luck? It's easy, but it's not obvious. The first thing you want to do is to change your relationship with yourself, be willing to take small risks to get you out of your comfort zone. The second thing you want to do is to change your relationship with other people. You need to understand that every  who helps you on your journey is playing a huge role in getting you to your goals. And if you don't show appreciation, not only are you not closing the loop, but you're missing an opportunity. And third, you want to change your relationship with ideas. Most people look at new ideas that come their way and they judge them. That's a great idea, or that's a terrible idea.  But it's actually much more nuanced. Ideas are neither good and bad. And in fact, in the seeds of terrible ideas are often something truly remarkable. Yes, sometimes people weren't born into terrible circumstances. And sometimes luck is a lightning bolt that hits us with something wonderful or something terrible. But the winds of luck are always there. And if you're willing to take some risks, if you're willing...  to really go out and show appreciation and you're willing to really look at ideas even if they're crazy through the lens of possibilities. You build a bigger and bigger sail to catch the winds of luck.

transcript: So you have a great idea that you want to pitch. Now what? Here are three steps from Ted Speaker, Mark Hirschenson to help you get started whether you're an entrepreneur or you're pitching a new project to your boss. Step one, know your audience. Your first instinct might be to think of yourself like your own nerves or how you're going to say what you want to say, but the secret to pitching successfully is to flip it outward. Who are you pitching to? What do they care about?  Step 2. Think about the hero's journey. Pitching is like telling a story. Introduce your idea by talking about the situation it will address. Create tension by showing the problems that exist without it and then show how your idea will save the day and be the ultimate resolution. Step 3. Call out your weakest point.  You might think it's best to gloss over your biggest weakness of your project, but talking about it head on before your audience asks about it will inspire confidence in you and what you have to offer. So if you have an idea that's been sitting in your drafts for way too long, use these three tips to help you bring it to life with confidence.

transcript: The thing that makes El Cap so intimidating is this year's scale of the wall. 3,000 feet of climbing represents thousands of distinct hand and foot movements, which is a lot to remember. Many of the moves I knew through sheer repetition. I'd climbed El Cap maybe 50 times over the previous decade with a rope. Once I found sequences that felt secure and repeatable, I had to memorize them.  I worked to cultivate that mindset through visualization. I'd imagine it all like a choreographed dance, thousands of feet up. The most difficult part of the whole route was called the Boulder problem. It was about 2,000 feet off the ground and consisted of the hardest physical moves on the whole route. An edge smaller than the width of a pencil.  but facing downward, so that I had to press up into with my thumb. The crux culminated in a karate kick with my left foot over to the inside of an adjacent corner. As I practiced the moves, my visualization turned to the emotional component of the potential solo. Basically, what if I got up there and it was too scary? That was the free curse of the fear. And I knew that I couldn't experience my perfect moment if I was afraid. I had to visualize and rehearse enough to remove all doubt. On June 3rd, 2017, I woke up early, ate my usual breakfast and musling fruit.  and made it to the base of the wall before sunrise and knew exactly what to do and how to do it. I had no doubts I just climbed right through. I rested for a moment below the boulder problem and then climbed it just as I had practiced so many times with the rope on. My foot shot across to the wall on the left without hesitation and I knew that I'd done it. I climbed with a smooth precision and enjoyed the sounds of the bird swooping around the cliff and then I reached the summit after three hours and 56 minutes of glorious climbing. It was the climb that I wanted and it felt like mastery.

transcript: Why does the feeling of time distort? Depending on the situation, our level of focus, our emotional state. It turns out that there are sensory neurons in our heart that fire signals to our brain to inform it about the state of the body. But they fire only at the time when the heart contracts and they remain silent when the heart is relaxed.  So the brain and the heart are in a constant rhythmic dance. Every beat makes the brain step into an active mode, priming us for action. And between the beats, the brain steps back into perceptual mode, priming us to take in information. What we found was that stimuli that occurred during heart's contraction were perceived to last shorter than stimuli that occurred between the beats when the heart was relaxed.  What this means is that the momentary state of the cart costs time to contract and expand within each heartbeat. It shows that perception of time is an embodied experience. It might be constructed in the brain, but it is molded by the body. Maybe that slowing of time you feel when you're bored?  Is there to actually expand your perception and allow your mind to wonder to something new?

The other time we pay attention to anxiety and consider it to be unhealthy.  is if the anxiety response is way too big for what's happened out of proportion to events.
